title: Production RAG Checklist: Check your understanding

questions.question: Six months after launch, users report your RAG system gives confident but wrong answers about your product's pricing. The pricing page was updated three months ago. Which production failure mode does this represent?
options: Retrieval miss: the pricing chunks are not indexed
Context confusion: the model is ignoring the retrieved pricing chunks
Stale knowledge: the index contains the old pricing page; the model faithfully cites outdated information
Generation failure: the model is hallucinating prices not present in any document
questions.explanation: The model is giving confident, grounded-sounding answers, which means retrieval and generation are working correctly. The problem is that the retrieved chunks accurately reflect the old pricing that was indexed before the update. This is the stale knowledge failure mode: the system is technically functioning, but the knowledge base hasn't been refreshed. The fix is a document refresh pipeline with staleness monitoring, not retrieval or prompting changes.

questions.question: You are launching a RAG system for a multi-tenant SaaS product where each company's documents should only be accessible to their own users. How should you enforce this isolation?
options: Add a system prompt instruction: 'Only show documents belonging to the current user's company'
Enforce access control at retrieval time using metadata filtering: filter by tenant ID before similarity search, never relying on the model to enforce data boundaries
Store each tenant's documents in a separate vector database instance
Encrypt each tenant's chunks: the model cannot read encrypted content
questions.explanation: System prompt instructions are not a security boundary: a jailbreak or confused retrieval can bypass them. The model must never be the sole enforcer of data access. Enforce isolation at the retrieval layer: filter by tenant_id in the metadata query before similarity search runs. A shared index with strict metadata filtering is a common production pattern; per-tenant indexes are also valid but have higher operational cost. Either approach keeps access control out of the model's hands.

questions.question: What is the correct approach when you want to upgrade the embedding model in a production RAG system?
options: Update the query embedding model first, then re-index documents incrementally
Re-embed and re-index the full document corpus with the new model before switching queries to it: mixing old and new embeddings produces meaningless similarity scores
Run both models in parallel for two weeks, then switch when users report better results
Embedding model upgrades don't require re-indexing if both models have the same output dimension
questions.explanation: Embedding models define their own vector spaces, documents embedded with model A and queries embedded with model B produce meaningless similarity scores (this is the production gotcha from module 2.2). An upgrade requires re-embedding the full corpus with the new model before any query uses it. The correct sequence: re-index all documents → validate recall@k on eval set → switch queries to new model → deprecate old index. Dimension matching is irrelevant, two different models with the same output dimension still produce incompatible vector spaces.

questions.question: Your RAG system's generation cost dominates the monthly bill. Which optimisation has the largest expected impact?
options: Reduce embedding API calls by caching common query embeddings
Switch from a managed vector database to a self-hosted solution to eliminate retrieval costs
Reduce the number of retrieved chunks from 10 to 3–5 and evaluate whether a fast/balanced model handles the task: generation input and output tokens dominate RAG costs
Compress chunk text before passing to the model to reduce input token count
questions.explanation: In a typical RAG request, embedding costs ~$0.00002 and vector DB retrieval is negligible, but generation of (system prompt + context + output) at frontier model prices can be 100–1000× the embedding cost. Reducing retrieved chunks from 10 to 4 cuts ~1,800 tokens of context per request. Right-sizing to a fast or balanced model (often 3–5× cheaper per token) can cut generation costs further. These two levers dwarf any savings from embedding caching or vector DB optimisation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a P0: must-have before launching a production RAG system?
options: Re-ranker configured and tested
RAGAS faithfulness scoring running on 100% of production traffic
Relevance threshold set so low-quality retrieved chunks are excluded before reaching the model, and grounding instruction in the system prompt with an explicit no-answer path
Multi-query retrieval enabled to maximise recall
questions.explanation: A relevance threshold prevents empty or irrelevant chunks from reaching the model (avoiding confident wrong answers from garbage retrieval). A grounding instruction prevents the model from blending retrieved content with training knowledge. An explicit no-answer path prevents hallucination when the knowledge base can't answer the question. These three together are the minimum safety net for a user-facing RAG system. A re-ranker and RAGAS monitoring are P1–P2; multi-query retrieval is a pattern to add after measuring that basic recall is insufficient.
